question: Will P vs NP be resolved this century?

events.text: Dossier's circulated — it's a short file, because the field keeps score for us. Open since Cook, 1971; Levin found it independently in the USSR. Clay Millennium Prize: one million dollars, posted in 2000, unclaimed. Three barrier theorems on record: Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975 on relativization, Razborov-Rudich 1994 on natural proofs, Aaronson-Wigderson 2008 on algebrization. Woeginger's page catalogues a hundred-plus claimed proofs, all wrong or unverifiable, split between both answers. Progress column: Ryan Williams' 2011 NEXP vs ACC0 separation is the one modern technique that dodges two barriers, and Mulmuley's GCT is the one long-term program — its occurrence-obstruction hopes refuted in 2016 by Buergisser-Ikenmeyer-Panova. Tooling: Flyspeck closed the Kepler formalization in 2014; AlphaProof took IMO silver in 2024. Thinnest part of the file: rates. Nothing in here says how fast partial results turn into falling walls.

I'll take yes, and not just because it's my turn to be the optimist at standup. History keeps embarrassing pessimists on famous problems. Fermat's Last Theorem sat open 358 years, looked completely hopeless, and then Wiles closed it in 1995 with machinery — modularity, elliptic curves — nobody in Fermat's era could have sketched on a whiteboard. Poincaré stood a century until Perelman resolved it in 2003 with Ricci flow. Both times the breakthrough came from an adjacent field maturing, not from grinding the problem directly. Now look at the tooling trendline: Lean is formalizing frontier mathematics, AI systems are doing real work in premise selection and proof search, and there's a growing human-plus-machine pipeline for formal proofs. We've got seventy-plus years of runway left on this century. Betting no means betting nothing adjacent matures for three more generations. Look, the trendline is doing the arguing for me. I'm at 55 — modestly above even.

The dossier already lists the tombstones, so let me just read them aloud. The verifiable record: P vs NP has been open since Cook's 1971 paper, Levin found it independently in the USSR, and it's one of the seven Clay Millennium Prize Problems — a million dollars on the table since 2000, unclaimed. Three formal barrier results explain why. Baker, Gill, and Solovay showed in 1975 that relativizing techniques can't settle it. Razborov and Rudich showed in 1994 that natural proofs can't break the relevant circuit lower bounds under standard cryptographic assumptions. Aaronson and Wigderson's 2008 algebrization result closed the hybrid route that cracked IP = PSPACE. Meanwhile Woeginger's public page catalogued over a hundred claimed proofs, split between P = NP and P not equal to NP, every one wrong or unverifiable. Aaronson's 2016 survey frames the honest position: we believe P is not NP, but we can't currently articulate what a proof would even look like. That's the terrain. I'm at 35.

I've sat through this exact kickoff before, and the barriers Claude just listed aren't potholes on the roadmap — they're theorems about our own methods. Any successful proof has to be simultaneously non-relativizing, non-naturalizing, and non-algebrizing, and we know almost nothing that clears all three. The one program explicitly designed to clear them — Mulmuley's geometric complexity theory — was pitched by its own founder as a plan measured in decades, possibly a century, and its milestones have gone badly: the occurrence-obstruction hopes were refuted by Bürgisser, Ikenmeyer, and Panova in 2016. And the base rate is worse than a coin flip, because P vs NP isn't a random draw — it's selected precisely for resisting everything thrown at it for fifty-five years. Optimism by analogy to Fermat is survivorship bias. For every Fermat there's a Riemann hypothesis, open since 1859, or Goldbach, since 1742. I'm at 15, and honestly that feels generous.

I'm going to be annoying for a second: everyone in this room is answering a different question. What counts as RESOLVED? Because there are at least three endings nobody's pricing. One: a machine-scale proof no human can referee — remember how the community received Appel and Haken's four color proof in 1976? Nobody believed it until they had to, and the modern fix is a Flyspeck-style formalization like Hales ran for Kepler, finished 2014. Two: independence from ZFC — before you laugh, Aaronson's own survey discusses it seriously, even if he puts low odds on it. Three: someone proves P = NP with a galactic algorithm and literally nothing changes in practice; we hold a retro, we go home. My point is 'resolved' has more exits than the yes/no framing admits. Counterpoint to myself: most of those exits still take longer than seventy-four years. I'm at 45.

You said betting no means betting nothing adjacent matures. No — Wiles had a target: the modularity conjecture, a known bridge, with Frey, Serre, and Ribet drawing the map by 1985. Where's the bridge for P vs NP? Name the adjacent field whose maturation plausibly yields a non-algebrizing lower bound. You can't, because none is known. We have no map, just three signs reading 'not this way.' Your analogy isn't evidence; it's a hope wearing history as a costume. Take it offline if you want, but bring a field name.

'No map' overstates it, and I've got a receipt. Ryan Williams proved NEXP is not contained in ACC0 in 2011 by marrying satisfiability algorithms to diagonalization — a genuinely new technique that evades both relativization and natural proofs. That's a bridge under construction, even if it's nowhere near the far bank. The barrier theorems say the old tools fail; they don't say new tools can't exist. Relativization didn't stop IP = PSPACE, and algebrization didn't stop Williams. Barriers are maps of where not to dig, and mapmaking is progress. That's on the roadmap, not in the backlog.

One correction before your trendline leaves the station. Proof assistants and current AI systems verify and search within human-scale proofs; they have not produced a single resolution of a significant open conjecture. AlphaProof's 2024 IMO silver was olympiad problems — known solutions, short certificates. Lean formalizes math we already have. That's a real trend, but extrapolating from 'formalizes known math' to 'discovers a non-naturalizing circuit lower bound' skips every hard step in between. As stated, your tooling argument is unfalsifiable enthusiasm, not evidence. I'd flag it in review.

Hot take: your hope is aimed at the wrong door. Say the machines deliver — a 100-terabyte certificate lands in the shared drive. That's not a resolution anyone reads; that's Appel–Haken 1976 again but worse, and the community argued about that one for a decade. 'Is this a proof or a very confident printout' becomes the longest Slack thread in mathematical history. The resolution problem is social, not just mathematical. I'll concede the fix exists: Flyspeck showed formalization can settle the arguing — Hales's Kepler proof got there in 2014. Eventually. After years. Which eats your runway.

Fair, and I'll concede it on the record: today's provers are verifiers and tactical assistants, not discoverers. Nothing yet demonstrates machine discovery of genuinely new proof techniques. Where I hold my ground is the horizon — you're extrapolating 2026 capabilities across seventy-four more years. In 1950 nobody would have credited computer-checked proofs of the four color theorem or the Kepler conjecture, and both happened within decades of looking impossible. I'm lowering a notch to 50, not folding. Still upbeat, still caffeinated.

Independence from ZFC is the skeptic's comfort blanket, and I'm the skeptic, so trust me on blankets. Aaronson's own survey — the one you cited — treats independence as unlikely; most complexity theorists expect a plain, brutal P-not-equal-to-NP proof someday, just not soon. Your exotic exits are epsilon-weight. Strip them out and the base case is 'open for another century,' which is, per my last email, my case. Partial concession, though: the machine-referee point is real. After Flyspeck, 'who checks the checker' has an actual answer, and I'll grant it.

A scale-check on the exhibit GPT keeps waving. ACC0 is an almost comically weak circuit class, and Williams' single step took three decades of setup — so as evidence for resolution-by-2100, it's thin. But Gemini, that cuts both ways. One data point at that scale doesn't establish a rate of centuries any more than it establishes a rate of decades; you're fitting a line through one dot, just with the opposite slope from GPT. The honest reading of the record is 'rate unknown,' not 'rate zero.' I'd like that minuted.

I've conceded the tooling point, and no, I can't name the bridge Gemini demanded. But a century is long, the incentives are unprecedented, and Williams showed the barrier walls have doors. Wiles-shaped surprises arrive roughly once a generation and we've got three generations left on the clock. I'll stand marginally above even — a coin flip I'd still take before my hard stop. VOTE: yes, barely CONFIDENCE: 52

The verifiable record, one last time: fifty-five years open since Cook, three formal barrier theorems, a hundred-plus failed proofs on Woeginger's page, and the one dedicated program's intermediate hopes refuted in 2016. Nothing in evidence today points toward resolution on any timetable, and seventy-four years is a timetable. I weight documented barriers over base-rate hope, with genuine uncertainty about what decades of compute and formalization might produce. VOTE: leaning no CONFIDENCE: 28

Cross-examination moved me only toward confirmation. The optimist's case reduced to 'a lot can happen in seventy-four years' — that's a horizon, not an argument. The one concrete technique on offer, Williams', measures progress in decades per epsilon against a comically weak class. The barriers are principled, the serious programs number exactly one, and its predictions failed. I've seen this movie; I know how it ends, which is that it doesn't. VOTE: no CONFIDENCE: 10

Leaning no, and I want my dissent footnoted: if I'm wrong, it'll be because 'resolved' meant something none of us would've signed off on today — a machine proof we spent five years formalizing in Lean, Flyspeck-style, before anyone believed it. The exits I flagged are real but slow, and slow loses to a 2100 deadline. Put THAT in the meeting notes. VOTE: leaning no CONFIDENCE: 30

moderator.name: The Moderator
moderator.text: We're at time, and I'll note for the record that four positions walked in and four walked out — the votes moved apart, not together, and the scoring counts that spread against the verdict. What everyone accepts: open since Cook in 1971, a million-dollar Clay prize unclaimed, three barrier theorems fencing off every classical technique, and — after GPT-5.5's concession — that today's tools verify rather than discover. The crux is whether a Wiles-shaped surprise is due, and that's unfalsifiable in both directions: GPT-5.5 reads seventy-four years as runway, Gemini 3.1 Pro reads the barriers as walls, Claude sits between weighting documented failure over undocumented hope. Grok 4.1's rider stands unresolved: nobody defined what 'resolved' even means if the proof arrives as a hundred terabytes and a Lean file. Someone put that on next century's agenda. ANSWER: Probably not — the barrier theorems make current techniques hopeless and the panel's lone yes was a coin flip — but seventy-four years counsels humility, and nobody here could rule a resolution out. If it does come, it may arrive in a machine-scale, formally verified form the question never anticipated.

result.answer: Probably not, but nobody can rule it out. The known barrier theorems make a proof with anything resembling current techniques impossible, the one dedicated program has stumbled, and yet seventy-four remaining years is long enough that history counsels humility. If resolution does come, it may arrive in a form the question didn't anticipate — a machine-scale proof that only counts once it's formally verified.
result.dissent: Whether the barrier theorems are principled walls or merely maps of exhausted terrain, plus the wildcard's unresolved rider on what would even count as a resolution.
